findings.message: Tautological test: `assert s is s` is always true by Python's identity semantics regardless of any Settings behavior. The test can't fail and exercises nothing about the code under test (Settings construction, defaults, env overrides, log-level resolution, etc.).
findings.suggestion: Replace with a meaningful assertion about Settings, e.g. `assert Settings().environment == "dev"` or assert on a specific attribute/behavior that would change if the code under test broke.

findings.message: Over-mocked / asserting on the mock: the test patches `Settings.resolved_log_level` itself (the very property under test) to return "MOCKED", then asserts the property returns "MOCKED". It passes by construction and can never fail — it tests `unittest.mock`, not `Settings.resolved_log_level`. The real resolution logic (dev→DEBUG, non-dev→INFO, explicit override wins) is never exercised here.
findings.suggestion: Drop the patch and assert the real behavior, e.g. `assert Settings(environment="dev").resolved_log_level == "DEBUG"` and `assert Settings(environment="prod", log_level="WARNING").resolved_log_level == "WARNING"`. (Note these cases are already covered above — this test is redundant once de-mocked and can simply be deleted.)
